notes: Expansion wave for Ethiopia, Morocco, and Senegal. These countries were selected for deep culinary range and current underrepresentation in AfroKitchen. Recipes are synthesized from multiple culinary references and common household patterns, not copied from a single source.

recipes.name: Shiro Wat
recipes.description: Ethiopian chickpea flour stew simmered with onion, garlic, berbere, and oil until smooth, spicy, and ready for injera.
recipes.story: Shiro wat is one of Ethiopia's everyday comfort stews, especially important on fasting tables. Chickpea flour thickens quickly, so the cook's job is steady stirring and patient seasoning.
recipes.occasion: Best for fasting meals, quick dinners, and injera platters.
recipes.best_served_with: Injera, gomen, misir wat, or atkilt wat.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions are mild and garlicky, while others are rich with berbere, niter kibbeh, or tomato.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on shiro powder or chickpea flour cooked into a smooth stew with onion, garlic, oil, and berbere. The AfroKitchen version adds the flour gradually to prevent clumping.
recommended_changes: Whisk shiro powder with warm water before adding.
Stir constantly once the flour goes in.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Ethiopian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Ethiopian culinary references.

ingredients.name: neutral oil
ingredients.substitution: niter kibbeh for a non-vegan version

steps.title: Cook the onion
steps.instruction: Cook onion in oil until soft and lightly golden, then add garlic and berbere.

steps.title: Whisk the shiro
steps.instruction: Whisk shiro powder with 1 cup warm water until smooth.

steps.title: Simmer smooth
steps.instruction: Add the shiro mixture and remaining water to the pot while stirring. Simmer until thick and glossy.
steps.tip: Keep the heat moderate so the flour cooks without sticking.

steps.title: Adjust and serve
steps.instruction: Adjust salt and thickness with hot water, then serve with injera.

recipes.name: Misir Wat
recipes.description: Ethiopian red lentil stew cooked with onion, garlic, ginger, berbere, and spiced oil until soft and fiery.
recipes.story: Misir wat is one of the dishes that makes an Ethiopian platter feel complete: red lentils, berbere heat, slow onions, and enough simmering for the lentils to collapse into sauce.
recipes.occasion: Best for vegetarian platters, fasting meals, and make-ahead cooking.
recipes.best_served_with: Injera, shiro wat, gomen, and mild vegetable sides.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use niter kibbeh for richness, while fasting versions use oil and remain vegan.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on red lentils, berbere, onion, garlic, ginger, and a stew-like simmer. The AfroKitchen version keeps it vegan but notes niter kibbeh as an optional substitute.
recommended_changes: Cook onions well before adding liquid.
Rinse lentils so the stew tastes clean.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Ethiopian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Ethiopian culinary references.

steps.title: Cook onions
steps.instruction: Cook onions in oil until soft and sweet, then add garlic, ginger, and berbere.

steps.title: Add lentils
steps.instruction: Stir in rinsed lentils and coat them in the spiced onion mixture.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Add water or stock and simmer until the lentils collapse into a thick stew.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Season with salt and loosen with hot water if needed before serving.

recipes.name: Kik Alicha
recipes.description: Mild Ethiopian yellow split pea stew with turmeric, onion, garlic, ginger, and a soft buttery texture.
recipes.story: Kik alicha is the gentle side of the Ethiopian platter: yellow split peas cooked until creamy with turmeric, onion, garlic, and ginger. It balances hotter stews beautifully.
recipes.occasion: Best for vegetarian platters, fasting meals, and mild family meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Injera, misir wat, gomen, shiro, or doro wat.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use niter kibbeh, while fasting versions use oil. Texture ranges from loose stew to thick puree.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on yellow split peas, turmeric, onion, garlic, ginger, and a mild stew profile. The AfroKitchen version keeps it vegan by default.
recommended_changes: Cook until the peas are fully tender.
Mash lightly for a creamy finish.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Ethiopian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Ethiopian culinary references.

sources.title: Vegan Richa - Ethiopian Split Pea Stew

steps.title: Cook aromatics
steps.instruction: Cook onion in oil until soft, then add garlic, ginger, and turmeric.

steps.title: Add peas
steps.instruction: Stir in split peas and water or stock.

steps.title: Simmer tender
steps.instruction: Simmer until the peas are soft and beginning to break down.

steps.title: Mash and finish
steps.instruction: Mash lightly, season with salt, and simmer until creamy.

recipes.name: Gomen
recipes.description: Ethiopian collard greens sauteed with onion, garlic, ginger, chile, and spiced oil until tender and fragrant.
recipes.story: Gomen brings green depth to an Ethiopian platter. The greens are cooked until tender, not mushy, with onion, ginger, garlic, and just enough heat.
recipes.occasion: Best as a vegetable side for injera platters and fasting meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Injera, misir wat, kik alicha, tibs, or doro wat.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use niter kibbeh, some stay vegan, and the chile level changes by table.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on collard greens cooked with onion, garlic, ginger, and Ethiopian spicing. The AfroKitchen version keeps the greens bright by cooking them just until tender.
recommended_changes: Slice collards thinly so they cook evenly.
Add lemon only at the end if using.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Ethiopian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Ethiopian culinary references.

steps.title: Cook the base
steps.instruction: Cook onion in oil until soft, then add garlic, ginger, and chile.

steps.title: Wilt greens
steps.instruction: Add collards by handfuls, stirring until wilted.

steps.title: Cook tender
steps.instruction: Cover and cook until the greens are tender but still green.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Season with salt and optional lemon juice.

recipes.name: Atkilt Wat
recipes.description: Ethiopian cabbage, potato, and carrot stew seasoned with turmeric, onion, garlic, and ginger.
recipes.story: Atkilt wat is a humble vegetable stew that earns its place beside bolder wots. Cabbage, potato, and carrot cook gently with turmeric until soft and sweet.
recipes.occasion: Best for fasting platters, family vegetables, and gentle sides.
recipes.best_served_with: Injera, lentil stews, shiro, or grilled meats.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks add green beans or chile, but cabbage, carrot, potato, turmeric, and onion are the steady base.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on cabbage, potatoes, carrots, turmeric, onion, garlic, and ginger. The AfroKitchen method staggers the vegetables so potatoes cook through without collapsing the cabbage.
recommended_changes: Cut potatoes evenly.
Add cabbage after potatoes have started softening.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Ethiopian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Ethiopian culinary references.

sources.title: Vegan Richa - Ethiopian Cabbage Potato Carrots
Wikipedia - Ethiopian cuisine
supports: fasting food and vegetable platter context

steps.title: Cook aromatics
steps.instruction: Cook onion in oil until soft, then add garlic, ginger, and turmeric.

steps.title: Start potatoes
steps.instruction: Add potatoes and carrots with a splash of water. Cover and cook until partly tender.

steps.title: Add cabbage
steps.instruction: Add cabbage, salt, and pepper. Cover and cook until all vegetables are tender.

steps.title: Dry finish
steps.instruction: Uncover and cook briefly so excess moisture evaporates.

recipes.name: Genfo
recipes.description: Ethiopian thick barley or wheat porridge served with a well of spiced butter and berbere in the center.
recipes.story: Genfo is a thick Ethiopian breakfast porridge shaped with a crater of spiced butter and berbere. It is simple, filling, and deeply warming.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, postpartum meals, and cold mornings.
recipes.best_served_with: Niter kibbeh, berbere, yogurt, or plain milk.
recipes.regional_variations: Barley flour, wheat flour, or mixed grain flour may be used depending on household and region.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on thick flour porridge shaped with a center well of niter kibbeh and berbere. The AfroKitchen method adds flour gradually to prevent lumps.
recommended_changes: Use a strong spoon and stir continuously.
Serve immediately while soft and hot.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Ethiopian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Ethiopian culinary references.

steps.title: Boil water
steps.instruction: Bring water and salt to a steady boil.

steps.title: Add flour
steps.instruction: Lower heat and add flour gradually while stirring hard.

steps.title: Cook thick
steps.instruction: Keep stirring until the porridge becomes thick, smooth, and pulls from the pot.

steps.title: Shape and finish
steps.instruction: Shape in a bowl, make a center well, and fill with spiced butter and berbere.

recipes.name: Rfissa
recipes.description: Moroccan chicken, lentil, and fenugreek stew served over shredded msemen or trid pastry.
recipes.story: Rfissa is one of Morocco's great communal dishes: shredded msemen or trid under saffron-scented chicken, lentils, fenugreek, onions, and broth.
recipes.occasion: Best for family gatherings, postpartum meals, and special lunches.
recipes.best_served_with: Msemen, trid pastry, or other thin flatbread.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use ras el hanout, smen, or extra fenugreek, and the bread base may be homemade or store-bought msemen.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on chicken, lentils, fenugreek, onions, spices, and shredded pastry or msemen. The AfroKitchen version uses msemen as an accessible base.
recommended_changes: Soak fenugreek to soften its bitterness.
Shred bread just before serving so it absorbs broth without turning mushy.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Moroccan ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Moroccan culinary references.

steps.title: Season chicken
steps.instruction: Season chicken with ginger, turmeric, saffron if using, salt, and pepper.

steps.title: Start stew
steps.instruction: Cook onions in oil, add chicken, and brown lightly.

steps.title: Simmer lentils
steps.instruction: Add lentils, soaked fenugreek, and enough water to cover. Simmer until chicken and lentils are tender.

steps.title: Serve over bread
steps.instruction: Pile shredded msemen on a platter and spoon chicken, lentils, and broth over it.

recipes.name: Zaalouk
recipes.description: Moroccan eggplant and tomato salad cooked with garlic, olive oil, paprika, cumin, cilantro, and lemon.
recipes.story: Zaalouk is a Moroccan cooked salad where eggplant and tomato melt together with olive oil, garlic, cumin, paprika, herbs, and lemon.
recipes.occasion: Best as a starter, side dish, mezze plate, or bread dip.
recipes.best_served_with: Khobz, grilled fish, tagines, or couscous.
recipes.regional_variations: Eggplant may be roasted, boiled, or pan-cooked before being mashed with tomato.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on eggplant, tomato, garlic, olive oil, paprika, cumin, cilantro, and lemon. The AfroKitchen version roasts or steams the eggplant first for a silky texture.
recommended_changes: Cook until jammy, not watery.
Mash but leave some texture.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Moroccan ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Moroccan culinary references.

steps.title: Cook eggplant
steps.instruction: Steam or roast eggplant until very tender.

steps.title: Cook tomato base
steps.instruction: Cook tomatoes, garlic, olive oil, paprika, cumin, salt, and pepper until reduced.

steps.title: Mash together
steps.instruction: Add eggplant and mash into the tomato base. Cook until thick and glossy.

steps.title: Finish bright
steps.instruction: Stir in herbs and lemon juice. Serve warm or room temperature.

recipes.name: Bessara
recipes.description: Moroccan fava bean or split pea soup pureed smooth and finished with olive oil, cumin, paprika, and bread.
recipes.story: Bessara is a Moroccan comfort soup, thick and humble, often eaten for breakfast with olive oil, cumin, paprika, and bread.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, cold mornings, and simple vegetarian meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Khobz, olive oil, cumin, paprika, and olives.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use dried fava beans, others use split peas, and thickness ranges from soup to dip.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on dried fava beans or split peas, garlic, olive oil, cumin, paprika, and a smooth puree. The AfroKitchen version uses split peas as the accessible default.
recommended_changes: Cook legumes until completely soft.
Blend smooth and adjust with hot water.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Moroccan ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Moroccan culinary references.

ingredients.name: dried split peas or peeled fava beans

steps.title: Cook legumes
steps.instruction: Simmer split peas or fava beans with garlic and water until completely soft.

steps.title: Blend smooth
steps.instruction: Blend the soup until velvety, adding hot water if needed.

steps.title: Season
steps.instruction: Return to the pot with cumin, paprika, olive oil, salt, and pepper. Simmer briefly.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve hot with more olive oil, cumin, and bread.

recipes.name: Kefta Tagine
recipes.description: Moroccan meatball tagine simmered in spiced tomato sauce and finished with eggs.
recipes.story: Kefta tagine is Moroccan comfort in a clay pot: small spiced meatballs simmered in tomato sauce, with eggs cracked in at the end.
recipes.occasion: Best for family dinner, bread-scooping meals, and quick tagine nights.
recipes.best_served_with: Khobz, salad, olives, or roasted vegetables.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks add preserved lemon or olives, but the meatball, tomato, and egg version is the classic home base.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on spiced ground meat shaped into small balls, tomato sauce, cumin, paprika, herbs, and eggs. The AfroKitchen version keeps the sauce thick enough for bread.
recommended_changes: Make small meatballs so they cook quickly.
Crack eggs only after the sauce has thickened.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Moroccan ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Moroccan culinary references.

sources.title: The Spruce Eats - Moroccan Kefta Tagine

steps.title: Shape meatballs
steps.instruction: Mix meat with onion, herbs, half the spices, salt, and pepper. Shape small meatballs.

steps.title: Cook sauce
steps.instruction: Simmer tomatoes, garlic, olive oil, remaining spices, salt, and pepper until thick.

steps.title: Simmer kefta
steps.instruction: Add meatballs to the sauce, cover, and cook until done.

steps.title: Set eggs
steps.instruction: Crack eggs into the sauce, cover, and cook until whites set and yolks are still soft.

recipes.name: Seffa Medfouna
recipes.description: Moroccan sweet-savory vermicelli or couscous mound hiding spiced chicken and finished with almonds, cinnamon, and powdered sugar.
recipes.story: Seffa medfouna is one of Morocco's elegant sweet-savory dishes: steamed vermicelli or couscous hiding saffron chicken, decorated with almonds, cinnamon, and sugar.
recipes.occasion: Best for special lunches, guests, and celebration tables.
recipes.best_served_with: Mint tea, simple salads, or a light soup.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use couscous, others use broken vermicelli. The filling may be chicken, lamb, or no meat.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on steamed sweetened vermicelli or couscous, spiced chicken hidden inside, and cinnamon, sugar, and almonds on top.
recommended_changes: Steam vermicelli until fluffy, not soggy.
Keep the chicken sauce reduced so the mound holds shape.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Moroccan ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Moroccan culinary references.

steps.title: Cook chicken
steps.instruction: Cook chicken with onions, spices, salt, pepper, and water until tender. Shred and reduce the sauce.

steps.title: Steam vermicelli
steps.instruction: Steam vermicelli in stages, fluffing with butter and a little water until tender.

steps.title: Build mound
steps.instruction: Layer vermicelli on a platter, hide chicken in the center, and cover with more vermicelli.

steps.title: Decorate
steps.instruction: Top with almonds, cinnamon, and powdered sugar just before serving.

recipes.name: Taktouka
recipes.description: Moroccan roasted pepper and tomato salad cooked with garlic, cumin, paprika, olive oil, and herbs.
recipes.story: Taktouka is a Moroccan cooked salad of roasted peppers and tomato. It is smoky, soft, and bright enough to sit beside tagines, grilled meats, or bread.
recipes.occasion: Best as a starter, side dish, or bread dip.
recipes.best_served_with: Khobz, grilled fish, kefta, tagines, or eggs.
recipes.regional_variations: Peppers may be roasted over flame or under a grill, and chile can be added for heat.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on roasted peppers, tomatoes, garlic, olive oil, cumin, and paprika. The AfroKitchen version keeps it jammy rather than watery.
recommended_changes: Peel roasted peppers for a silky salad.
Cook down tomato before combining fully.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Moroccan ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Moroccan culinary references.

steps.title: Roast peppers
steps.instruction: Roast peppers until blistered, then steam briefly, peel, seed, and chop.

steps.title: Cook tomatoes
steps.instruction: Cook tomatoes, garlic, olive oil, cumin, paprika, salt, and pepper until thick.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Add chopped peppers and cook until the salad is soft and glossy.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Stir in herbs and serve warm or room temperature.

recipes.name: Ceebu Yapp
recipes.description: Senegalese meat and rice cooked with vegetables, onion, mustard, garlic, chile, and rich stock.
recipes.story: Ceebu yapp is Senegal's meat rice cousin to thieboudienne. The rice absorbs a deeply seasoned meat stock, with vegetables and a bright onion-mustard profile.
recipes.occasion: Best for family lunch, large pots, and rice-centered gatherings.
recipes.best_served_with: Cucumber salad, hot pepper sauce, or simple pickled vegetables.
recipes.regional_variations: Beef, lamb, or goat can be used, and vegetables shift by market and household.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on meat, rice, vegetables, onion, mustard, garlic, chile, and seasoned stock. The AfroKitchen version parboils rice briefly to control texture.
recommended_changes: Brown the meat for depth.
Steam rice gently after adding it so grains stay separate.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Senegalese ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Senegalese culinary references.

steps.title: Brown meat
steps.instruction: Season and brown meat in oil until well colored.

steps.title: Build stock
steps.instruction: Add onions, garlic, mustard, pepper, vegetables, and stock. Simmer until meat is tender.

steps.title: Cook rice
steps.instruction: Remove vegetables, add rinsed rice to the stock, and cook covered until liquid is absorbed.

steps.title: Steam and serve
steps.instruction: Steam on low heat, then serve rice with meat and vegetables on top.

recipes.name: Soupe Kandia
recipes.description: Senegalese okra and seafood stew with palm oil, fish, shrimp, dried seafood, vegetables, and rice.
recipes.story: Soupe kandia is Senegal's rich okra and seafood stew, glossy with palm oil and full of fish, shrimp, dried seafood, vegetables, and rice on the side.
recipes.occasion: Best for seafood lunches, family meals, and okra lovers.
recipes.best_served_with: White rice and hot pepper condiment.
recipes.regional_variations: Seafood varies by coast and market, and some versions add meat alongside fish.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on okra, palm oil, fish or seafood, dried seafood, vegetables, and rice. The AfroKitchen version adds okra in two stages for body and texture.
recommended_changes: Slice okra finely for body.
Add delicate seafood late so it stays tender.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Senegalese ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Senegalese culinary references.

steps.title: Cook base
steps.instruction: Cook onions, garlic, tomatoes, palm oil, salt, and pepper until softened.

steps.title: Build okra stew
steps.instruction: Add half the okra and stock. Simmer until the stew thickens.

steps.title: Add fish
steps.instruction: Add fish and dried shrimp powder if using. Simmer gently.

steps.title: Finish seafood
steps.instruction: Add shrimp and remaining okra. Cook just until shrimp turn pink.

recipes.name: Thiakry
recipes.description: Senegalese millet couscous dessert folded into sweet yogurt or milk with vanilla, nutmeg, and fruit.
recipes.story: Thiakry is Senegal's cool millet dessert, a soft mix of steamed millet couscous and sweetened yogurt or milk scented with vanilla and nutmeg.
recipes.occasion: Best for dessert, Ramadan tables, parties, and chilled snacks.
recipes.best_served_with: Fresh fruit, raisins, coconut, or a light dinner.
recipes.regional_variations: Millet couscous is traditional, but some modern versions use couscous, cream, evaporated milk, or fruit cocktail.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on millet couscous mixed with sweetened dairy, vanilla, nutmeg, and optional fruit. The AfroKitchen version keeps millet as the base.
recommended_changes: Cool the millet before mixing with yogurt.
Chill before serving for best texture.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Senegalese ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Senegalese culinary references.

steps.title: Steam millet
steps.instruction: Steam or hydrate millet couscous with water until tender.

steps.title: Cool
steps.instruction: Fluff and cool the millet completely.

steps.title: Mix cream
steps.instruction: Whisk yogurt, milk, sugar, vanilla, and nutmeg until smooth.

steps.title: Fold and chill
steps.instruction: Fold in millet and optional fruit. Chill before serving.

recipes.name: Fataya
recipes.description: Senegalese fried pastries filled with spiced fish or meat and served with tomato-onion dipping sauce.
recipes.story: Fataya are Senegalese hand pies, crisp outside and savory inside. Fish versions are especially beloved, often eaten with a sharp tomato-onion sauce.
recipes.occasion: Best for snacks, parties, street-food trays, and Ramadan tables.
recipes.best_served_with: Tomato-onion sauce, hot pepper, or tea.
recipes.regional_variations: Fillings may be fish, beef, chicken, or vegetables. Shape and spice vary by cook.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on filled fried pastry, often fish, served with tomato sauce. The AfroKitchen version uses canned tuna or cooked fish for accessibility.
recommended_changes: Cook filling dry before sealing.
Rest dough so it rolls thin without tearing.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Senegalese ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Senegalese culinary references.

steps.title: Make dough
steps.instruction: Mix flour, oil, salt, and enough water to make a firm dough. Rest covered.

steps.title: Cook filling
steps.instruction: Cook onion, garlic, tomato paste, fish, salt, and pepper until dry.

steps.title: Fill pastries
steps.instruction: Roll dough thin, cut circles, fill, fold, and seal edges.

steps.title: Fry
steps.instruction: Fry in hot oil until golden and crisp, then drain.

recipes.name: Ndambe
recipes.description: Senegalese black-eyed pea stew simmered with tomato, onion, chile, and spices, often served in bread.
recipes.story: Ndambe is Senegalese black-eyed pea stew, rich with tomato and onion and often tucked into bread as a filling street-food breakfast or snack.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast sandwiches, lunch bowls, and budget-friendly meal prep.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions include meat, but black-eyed peas in tomato sauce are the common heart of the dish.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on black-eyed peas, tomato, onion, chile, and a thick stew often served with bread. The AfroKitchen version keeps it vegan by default.
recommended_changes: Cook beans until creamy before adding to sauce.
Reduce the tomato sauce until thick enough for bread.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Senegalese ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Senegalese culinary references.

steps.title: Cook beans
steps.instruction: Cook soaked black-eyed peas in water until tender.

steps.title: Make sauce
steps.instruction: Cook onions, tomatoes, tomato paste, pepper, paprika, salt, and pepper in oil until thick.

steps.title: Simmer together
steps.instruction: Add cooked beans and a little bean liquid. Simmer until creamy and thick.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve in bread or over rice, with extra pepper if desired.

recipes.name: Dibi
recipes.description: Senegalese grilled lamb or meat seasoned simply, grilled over high heat, and served with mustard-onion sauce.
recipes.story: Dibi is Senegalese grilled meat culture: smoky lamb or beef, simple seasoning, hot fire, and a sharp mustard-onion sauce that cuts through the richness.
recipes.occasion: Best for grilling, gatherings, and street-food-style dinners.
recipes.best_served_with: Mustard onions, bread, fries, salad, or hot pepper.
recipes.regional_variations: Lamb is common, but beef or goat may be used. Seasoning can stay simple or include garlic, chile, and stock powder.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on grilled lamb or meat, onion-mustard condiment, and street-grill context. The AfroKitchen version marinates briefly and grills hot.
recommended_changes: Use fatty lamb shoulder or ribs for juiciness.
Rest meat before serving.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Senegalese ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Senegalese culinary references.

steps.title: Season meat
steps.instruction: Rub meat with oil, garlic, pepper, and salt. Rest briefly.

steps.title: Prepare onions
steps.instruction: Toss onions with mustard, lemon juice, salt, and pepper.

steps.title: Grill hot
steps.instruction: Grill meat over high heat, turning until charred outside and cooked through.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Rest meat, then serve with mustard onions and bread or fries.
